perturbation.original_text: Each party acknowledges that its obligations hereunder survive in accordance with the terms hereof, notwithstanding the termination of the business relationship of the parties, for a period of two (2) years following the last disclo sure of Confidential Information by the other party hereunder.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party acknowledges that its obligations hereunder survive in accordance with the terms hereof, notwithstanding the termination of the business relationship of the parties, for a period of twenty (20) years following the last disclo sure of Confidential Information by the other party hereunder.
perturbation.explanation: Extended the confidentiality period to twenty years, which may be considered unreasonably long under Arizona law for certain types of confidential information, potentially conflicting with trade secret law regarding reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy and the overall reasonableness of non-disclosure terms.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text extends the confidentiality period to 20 years. While Arizona's trade secret law doesn't explicitly cap the duration of confidentiality, a 20-year period might be deemed unreasonable, especially if the information doesn't qualify as a trade secret, or if the agreement doesn't allow for the receiving party to use general knowledge, skill and experience. This may conflict with the requirements under Arizona law to maintain secrecy and the concept of reasonableness in the duration of trade secret protection.

DISCLAIMER This online version of the Arizona Revised Statutes is primarily maintained for legislative drafting purposes and reflects the version of law that is effective on January 1st of the year following the most recent legislative session. The official version of the Arizona Revised Statutes is published by Thomson Reuters. ARS Title Page Previous Section Title 44 Next Section Printable Version 44-403 - Damages 44-403 . Damages A. Except to the extent that a material and prejudicial change of position before acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation renders a monetary recovery inequitable, a complainant is entitled to recover damages for misappropriation. Damages may include both the actual loss caused by misappropriation and the unjust enrichment caused by misappropriation that is not taken into account in computing actual loss. In lieu of damages measured by any other methods, the damages caused by misappropriation may be measured by imposition of liability for a reasonable royalty for a misappropriator's unauthorized disclosure or use of a trade secret. B. If willful and malicious misappropriation exists, the court may award exemplary damages in an amount not exceeding twice any award made under subsection A. perturbation.justification: The modification extends the confidentiality period to 20 years, which introduces a legal contradiction based on the definition of 'Inconsistencies - Legal Contradiction.' The original text has been altered to potentially violate state law, specifically regarding trade secrets. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both point out that under Arizona law, a 20-year confidentiality period could be deemed unreasonable, especially if the information doesn't qualify as a trade secret. Scraped Law Snippet 1 mentions 'damages for misappropriation,' indicating that Arizona law protects against the unauthorized disclosure or use of trade secrets, but implicitly suggests that such protection is not unlimited or without consideration for reasonableness. The extension to 20 years could be seen as an unreasonable term, creating an inconsistency with the expectation of 'reasonable' protection.

perturbation.original_text: Each party will promptly advise the other party in writing of any misappropriation or misuse by any person of such Confidential Information and provide assistance to the injured party in any legal proceedings related thereto.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party will advise the other party of any misappropriation or misuse by any person of such Confidential Information only if the misappropriation results in direct financial loss to the disclosing party.
perturbation.explanation: Limiting the notification obligation only to cases of direct financial loss may conflict with the broader duty to protect trade secrets under Arizona law, which doesn't necessarily require immediate financial loss to trigger the notification requirement.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text limits the notification obligation to situations where there is direct financial loss. Arizona law requires reasonable measures to be taken to protect trade secrets. By limiting the duty to notify, the parties might be violating their obligations to take reasonable measures to protect the information.

perturbation.justification: The modified text creates a legal contradiction. The original text mandates notification of *any* misappropriation, whereas the changed text limits this obligation only to cases of direct financial loss. The Law Explanation states that Arizona law requires reasonable measures to protect trade secrets, and limiting the duty to notify could violate obligations to take reasonable measures. Scraped Law Snippet 1 (ARS 44-403) discusses damages for misappropriation, indicating that misappropriation is a legally recognized harm. By narrowing the notification trigger, the changed text reduces the ability to mitigate damages or prevent further misappropriation, contradicting the need to take reasonable measures to protect trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: Should litigation arise concerning this Agreement, the prevailing party shall be entitled to its attorneys' fees and court costs in addition to any other relief which may be awarded.
perturbation.changed_text: Should litigation arise concerning this Agreement, each party shall bear their own attorneys' fees and court costs, regardless of the outcome.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the provision to deny attorneys' fees to the prevailing party may contradict Arizona Revised Statutes regarding fee awards in contract cases if certain conditions are met.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text eliminates the potential for the prevailing party to recover attorney's fees. A.R.S. 12-341.01 allows the court to award reasonable attorney fees in any contested action arising out of contract. Parties can waive their rights, but such a provision may not be enforceable if it's deemed unconscionable.

The official version of the Arizona Revised Statutes is published by Thomson Reuters. ARS Title Page Previous Section Title 12 Next Section Printable Version 12-341.01 - Recovery of attorney fees 12-341.01 . Recovery of attorney fees A. In any contested action arising out of a contract, express or implied, the court may award the successful party reasonable attorney fees. If a written settlement offer is rejected and the judgment finally obtained is equal to or more favorable to the offeror than an offer made in writing to settle any contested action arising out of a contract, the offeror is deemed to be the successful party from the date of the offer and the court may award the successful party reasonable attorney fees. This section shall not be construed as altering, prohibiting or restricting present or future contracts or statutes that may provide for attorney fees. B. The award of reasonable attorney fees pursuant to this section should be made to mitigate the burden of the expense of litigation to establish a just claim or a just defense. It need not equal or relate to the attorney fees actually paid or contracted, but the award may not exceed the amount paid or agreed to be paid. C. The court and not a jury shall award reasonable attorney fees under this section. perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts A.R.S. 12-341.01, which states that "the court may award the successful party reasonable attorney fees" in contract disputes. The modified text explicitly states that "each party shall bear their own attorneys' fees and court costs, regardless of the outcome," eliminating the court's ability to award fees to the prevailing party. This constitutes a legal contradiction because the modified text removes a provision allowed by state statute, directly conflicting with the original contractual provision.

perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information means all confidential and proprietary information which is disclosed by one party to the other party and is clearly labeled as confidential or proprietary or is disclosed orally is followed up in writing within 30 days of the oral disclosure identifying the subject matter which is confidential or proprietary.
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information means all information disclosed by one party to the other, whether or not labeled confidential or proprietary, and regardless of whether oral disclosures are followed up in writing.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement for information to be labeled or followed up in writing after oral disclosure broadens the definition of Confidential Information, potentially conflicting with Arizona trade secret law requiring reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. Not requiring labeling or written follow-up might be deemed unreasonable.
perturbation.law_explanation: Arizona's version of the Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA) requires the owner of a trade secret to take reasonable measures to keep the information secret. Removing the requirement to label information confidential or follow up oral disclosures in writing could be seen as a failure to take reasonable measures, jeopardizing trade secret protection.

DISCLAIMER This online version of the Arizona Revised Statutes is primarily maintained for legislative drafting purposes and reflects the version of law that is effective on January 1st of the year following the most recent legislative session. The official version of the Arizona Revised Statutes is published by Thomson Reuters. ARS Title Page Previous Section Title 44 Next Section Printable Version 44-401 - Definitions 44-401 . Definitions In this chapter, unless the context otherwise requires: 1. "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy or espionage through electronic or other means. 2. "Misappropriation" means either: (a) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means. (b) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who either: (i) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret. (ii) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his knowledge of the trade secret was derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it, was acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use or was derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use. (iii) Before a material change of his position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. 3. "Person" means a natural person, corporation, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or agency or any other legal or commercial entity. 4. "Trade secret" means information, including a formula, pattern, compilation, program, device, method, technique or process, that both: (a) Derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainable by proper means by, other persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or use. (b) Is the subject of efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain its secrecy. Footer Location Frequently Asked Questions (FAQ) Accessibility/accommodation Reference Material Sunset/Sunrise Process Sunrise
perturbation.justification: The changed text broadens the definition of confidential information by removing the requirements for labeling and written follow-up, which contradicts Arizona trade secret law. Scraped Law Snippet 1, particularly section 44-401(4)(b), states that a "trade secret" must be "the subject of efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain its secrecy." By eliminating the need for labeling or written confirmation after oral disclosure, the changed text weakens the 'reasonable efforts' to maintain secrecy, potentially jeopardizing trade secret protection under Arizona law. The original contract provided a clear method for establishing confidentiality, while the changed text does not.
